title: Modal Verbs — can, could, should, must
description: Express ability, advice, obligation, and possibility using modal verbs in everyday situations.

items.back: I can swim. Can I help you?
items.example: She can speak three languages fluently.

items.back: I could run fast as a kid. Could you help me?
items.example: Could you pass the salt, please?

items.back: You should rest. You shouldn't smoke.
items.example: You look tired — you should get some sleep.
You must be 18 to enter this venue.
She has to wear a uniform at work.
Students must not use phones during the exam.

items.back: You don't have to come if you're busy.
items.example: It's optional — you don't have to attend.

items.back: It might rain. He may be late.
items.example: She might call later — I'm not sure.

items.back: I would love to. Would you like some coffee?
items.example: Would you like me to help?

items.prompt: She ___ speak French, Spanish, and English — she grew up in three countries.
items.explanation: 'Can' expresses current ability: she can speak three languages.

items.prompt: You look really tired. You ___ get some rest tonight.
items.explanation: 'Should' gives advice or a recommendation: you should rest.

items.prompt: You ___ park here — it's a fire lane. You'll get a fine.
items.explanation: 'Must not' is a strong prohibition: parking here is not allowed.

items.prompt: It's a public holiday — you ___ go to work today.
items.explanation: 'Don't have to' = no obligation. The day off means work isn't required.

items.prompt: ___ you open the window? It's quite warm in here.
items.explanation: 'Could you…?' is a polite request. 'Can you…?' is also correct but less formal.

activities.title: Complete with the correct modal verb

items.sentence: You ___ eat more vegetables. It's good for your health.
I ___ drive when I was twelve — I was too young.
You ___ use your phone during the exam — it's strictly forbidden.
___ I use your charger for a moment?
She ___ be home — her lights are off and the car is gone.
